prompt: A demographic model was fitted to the joint SFS of indica and temperate japonica using fastsimcoal2. The best-fit model parameters and confidence intervals are shown. Interpret the demographic history and evaluate which parameters are well-constrained.
context:
fastsimcoal2 best-fit demographic model (indica vs temperate japonica):

Model: Ancestral split with bottleneck + exponential recovery

Parameter estimates (95% CI from 100 bootstrap replicates):

  Parameter                              Estimate     95% CI
  N_ancestral (wild O. rufipogon)        82,400       [61,200 - 112,800]
  T_split (indica-japonica, generations) 8,200        [6,400 - 11,800]
  N_indica_bottleneck                    4,100        [2,800 - 6,200]
  N_indica_current                       48,200       [34,100 - 68,400]
  N_japonica_bottleneck                  1,200        [680 - 2,100]
  N_japonica_current                     8,400        [5,200 - 14,100]
  T_bottleneck_duration (generations)    2,400        [1,200 - 4,800]
  Migration_indica->japonica (per gen)   2.4e-5       [8.2e-6 - 7.1e-5]
  Migration_japonica->indica (per gen)   1.8e-5       [4.1e-6 - 5.8e-5]

Log-likelihood: -142,841
AIC: 285,700

Assuming 1 generation/year for rice.
correct_answer: The model describes the independent domestication and subsequent divergence of indica and japonica: (1) ANCESTRAL POPULATION: N_ancestral=82,400 represents the effective size of the wild O. rufipogon progenitor population. This is well-constrained (CI ratio ~1.8x) and consistent with published estimates of wild rice Ne. (2) SPLIT TIME: 8,200 generations ago (~8,200 years ago at 1 gen/year). This is consistent with archaeological evidence of rice domestication ~8,000-10,000 years ago, though the wide CI [6,400-11,800] reflects difficulty in precisely dating the split from genetic data alone. (3) BOTTLENECK SEVERITY: Japonica experienced a much more severe bottleneck (N=1,200, 69x reduction from ancestral) than indica (N=4,100, 20x reduction). This is consistent with all other diversity metrics (pi, Tajima's D, SFS shape). The japonica bottleneck CI is wide (680-2,100), indicating this parameter is harder to estimate precisely. (4) RECOVERY: Both populations recovered substantially -- indica to 48,200 (12x from bottleneck) and japonica to 8,400 (7x). Indica's larger current Ne reflects its wider geographic range and larger breeding population. (5) MIGRATION: Both migration rates are very low (2e-5 per generation) but significantly non-zero, indicating limited but ongoing gene flow between indica and japonica. The rates are symmetric, suggesting no strong directional bias in introgression. The wide CIs (8-fold range) indicate migration is the hardest parameter to estimate. WELL-CONSTRAINED: N_ancestral, N_indica_current. POORLY CONSTRAINED: T_bottleneck_duration (4x CI range), migration rates (8x CI range) -- these co-vary and are inherently difficult to separate from the SFS alone.
rubric: Full marks (3): Must interpret each major parameter with biological context, compare bottleneck severities between subpopulations, evaluate which parameters are well vs poorly constrained from CI widths, and note the archaeological consistency of the split time. Mostly correct (2): Interprets most parameters but missing the CI evaluation or one major component. Partial (1): Describes parameter values without biological interpretation. Incorrect (0): Misinterprets the model direction (e.g., claims japonica had a less severe bottleneck).
notes: Tests interpretation of demographic model outputs -- a key skill for understanding population history from genomic data.
